title: Adhesive on Carpet
stainChemistry: Adhesive residue (tape, sticker, glue) contains synthetic polymers (acrylate, rubber, vinyl) that bond to carpet through a combination of mechanical adhesion and Van der Waals forces. Different adhesive types require different solvents. Rubber-based adhesives dissolve in petroleum solvents; acrylic adhesives respond to alcohols; cyanoacrylate (super glue) requires acetone.
whyThisWorks: POG or citrus-based solvent softens and dissolves the polymer adhesive on carpet. The solvent breaks the Van der Waals bonds between adhesive and fiber. For cyanoacrylate, acetone (where fiber-safe) is required — it's the only effective solvent. Mechanical removal after softening prevents fiber damage. Oil-based products (even cooking oil) can soften many adhesives as a first step.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Remove loose material mechanically — brush, scrape, or vacuum as appropriate
2. Apply NSD to the stain area — 5-10 min dwell time
3. Apply appropriate secondary agent based on stain response — 5-10 min dwell time
4. Flush with cool water. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse — 1-2 min
warnings: Always test on hidden area first

steps: 1. Remove loose material — brush, blot, or scrape as appropriate
2. Apply Dawn dish soap mixed with cold water — 5-10 min dwell time
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat if needed
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 attempts. Take to a professional for specialized treatment
warnings: Always test cleaning solution on a hidden area first
Air dry until stain is confirmed gone — heat sets most stains

neverDo: No chlorine bleach on colored carpet
Avoid over-wetting — causes backing damage and mold
fiberSensitivities: Most synthetic carpet (nylon/polyester) tolerates standard chemistry
Always blot from outside in to prevent spreading
Extract excess moisture to prevent backing damage

escalation.when: If adhesive stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is adhesive on carpet, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaning service with truck-mount extraction

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
